content.full_text:
SUPERSEDE!
bc. 1io.I(X’./)
Honorable Mary I. Winters
Counci liuember
City of Colusa
155 Jay Street
Colusa,
CA
95932
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-374
Dear Councilmember Winters:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Please note that our advice is limited to the provisions of
the Act.
We cannot provide advice about other conflict-of-
interest laws which may be applicable.
In addition, this letter
is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does not
act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
Is your adult son a member of your “immediate family”
for purposes of the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act?
2.
Is your son a potentially disqualifying source of income
by virtue of a personal loan you have made to him, with respect to
decisions concerning the city’s fire department where your son is
employed?
3.
Where you do have a conflict of interest,
may you attend
open or closed sessions of the city where information regarding a
decision for which you are disqualified is discussed?
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
- 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 7,
1994
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—94—374
Page
2
CONCLUSIONS
1 and 2.
Your adult son is not a member of your “immediate
family” within the meaning of the Act.
Moreover, your son is not
a potentially disqualifying source of income of yours by virtue of
the personal loan you made to him.
Therefore,
you are not
required to disqualify yourself from participating in city council
decisions concerning the fire department where your son is
employed.
3.
You may not attend a closed session of the city council
concerning a matter for which you are disqualified,
and materials
presented to the city council for a closed session of the city
council may not be provided to you when you are disqualified with
respect to that matter.
However, you are not precluded from being
present at an open meeting of the city council.
Except for
confidential material, material scheduled for discussion at an
open session of the city council need not be withheld from you
where you are disqualified from participating in a city council
decision.
FACTS
You are a councilmember for the City of Colusa.
You have a
son who is over 21 years old who is employed by the City of Colusa
as a fireman.
You have made a personal loan to your son in an
amount which exceeds $250 which will be paid off in two years.
You are seeking advice to determine whether you are precluded from
participating in city decisions concerning the fire department
where your son is employed.
Specifically,
you are concerned about
collective bargaining negotiations with the union representing the
fire department.
In a telephone conversation with you on
November 28,
1994,
you also asked whether you may attend closed
sessions where you are disqualified from a city matter.
ANALYSIS
For purposes of providing general guidance regarding the
application of the conflict-of-interest laws under the Act,
we.
have summarized below the conflict—of—interest provisions which
may be applicable to you and also addressed your specific
questions.
The primary purpose for the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Act is to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from
bias caused by their own financial interests.
In furtherance of
this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.


Our File No. A-94-374
Page
3
As a member of the Colusa City Council, you are a public
official under the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Thus,
you may not use
your official position to influence a governmental decision in
which you know or have reason to know you have a financial
interest.
Economic Interests
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a
member of the official’s immediate family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.


Our File No.
A—94—374
Page 4
Under your facts, to determine whether you must disqualify
yourself from participating in a governmental decision pursuant to
Section 87103,
you are required to consider the foreseeable
material financial effect of any pending and future governmental
decision on your sources of income and on members of your
immediate family.
“Immediate family” is defined in Section 82029 as the
official’s spouse and dependent children.
A child is considered a
“dependent child” for purposes of the Act if the child is under 18
years of age and the public official is entitled to claim the
dependent child as such for income tax purposes.
(Tremlett Advice
Letter,
No. 1-89-386; Lyon Advice Letter,
No. A-93-325.)
According to your facts, your adult son is not a dependent child.
Therefore, your son is not a member of your “immediate family”
under this definition which will require your disqualification
under the Act.
You have made an unsecured loan to your son in an amount
exceeding $250.
The term “income” as used in the Act includes
both gifts and loans.
(Section 82030.)
Thus, the question you
pose is whether his loan payments to you constitute a potentially
disqualifying source of income within the meaning of Section
87103(c), with respect to city decisions concerning the fire
department where he is employed.
Under the Act, .Section 82030(b) (9)
excludes loans among
family members from the definition of income.
For example,
we
have advised that repayment of a loan to a public official by a
family member is not reportable.
(Hughes Memo to File, Advice
No.
M—83-055.)
2
This would include loans to a public official’s
child.
(Section 8203 0(b) (9).)
Thus, the unsecured personal loan
3
from you to your son does not constitute “income” within the
meaning of Section 87103(c),
and repayment of that loan does not
subject you to disqualification from city decisions concerning the
city’s fire department.
However,
you may be disqualified if a city decision will
result in your personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities,
or those of your immediate family, increasing or decreasing by at
least $250.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
Therefore,
you must
2
However,
we have also advised that where a public official’s
son owned a plumbing business as a sole proprietor, both the son
and his business were considered sources of income to the official
where the official had loaned money to the son’s business.
(Allen
Advice Letter,
No.
1—90-384.)
This letter does not appear to
apply to your facts.
Moreover,
an unsecured personal loan is not a reportable
investment under the Act.
(Section 82034;
Schimandle Advice
Letter,
No.
1—92-463.)


Our File No.
A-94—374
Page 5
determine whether a particular city decision may have such an
effect on you.
Reasonably Foreseeable and Material Financial Effect
Where an economic interest exists which may require your
disqualification, you must then determine whether the effect of a
decision is reasonably foreseeable.
It is reasonably foreseeable
if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downev Community Development Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989-
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra at 823.)
Then,
you must determine if the decision has a material
financial effect on your interests.
Regulation 18702 sets forth
the guidelines for determining whether an official’s economic
interest (i.e., interest in real property, business entity, or
source of income)
in a decision is “materially” affected as
required by Section 87103.
If your economic interest is directly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.1
(copy enclosed) provides the
appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
However,
when
your economic interests will be affected indirectly by a decision,
the appropriate standards for determining materiality are those of
Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6
(copies enclosed).
Finally,
even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision is material, disqualification is
required only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally, or on a significant segment of the public
generally.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18703.)
Closed Sessions
You have also asked whether you may be present at either open
or closed sessions of the city.
Unless you have a disqualifying
conflict of interest,
you may be present at either open or closed
sessions of the city.
However,
we have interpreted the case of
Hamilton v.
Town of Los Gatos
(1989)
213 Cal.App.3d 1050 to
preclude a disqualified councilmember from attending a closed
session of the city council.
However,
a councilinember’s presence
at an open meeting is not precluded.
(Armas Advice Letter,
No.
A—90—499a; Haile Advice Letter,
No.
A—90-499b.)
We have also advised that if information in written form was
presented to the city council to enable them to discuss an issue
scheduled for a closed session of the city council, the Hamilton
case could be read to preclude providing the information to the


Our File No. A-94-374
Page
6
disqualified councilmember.
However, provision of non—
confidential material scheduled for discussion at an open session
of the city council to a disqualified official did not appear to
be prohibited by the Hamilton case.
(Haile Advice Letter, supra.)
Under your facts, your son does not constitute a
disqualifying economic interest of yours.
Therefore, provided no
other disqualifying economic interest exists,
you may attend
either open or closed sessions concerning the city’s fire
department.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosures
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.
